entry.title: Strategic Change and SAP SuccessFactors: Why Senior HR Leaders Choose Max Success Plans
entry.content:
When we speak to Senior HR leaders they often say that they are being asked to do something fundamentally difficult: drive continuous organisational change while the ground beneath the organisation keeps shifting. They’re expected to modernise HR operations, harmonise workforce data, introduce AI responsibly, and enable faster workforce decisions - all without destabilising day‑to‑day operations or eroding trust.

The challenge isn’t recognising that transformation is ongoing. The harder question is how to structure the organisation around that reality so the HR platform can keep evolving: how to keep decisions coherent across regions and partners, how to maintain trust in data as the landscape changes, and how to adopt innovation without accumulating risk or complexity that slows the business down later.

For many senior HR leaders, this tension becomes most visible at the point where operational decisions begin to carry strategic weight. Choices about data models, process design, governance, or AI adoption no longer affect just HR efficiency; they shape how confidently the organisation can plan, respond, and grow. At that level, HR technology stops being a delivery concern and becomes a question of organisational capability.

That shift changes the role SAP SuccessFactors plays in the enterprise, and it changes what leaders need from the engagement model around it.

From HR Delivery to Strategic Enablement

At leadership level, HR technology decisions quickly move beyond implementation performance. The focus shifts to organisational capability: data quality leaders can trust, processes that scale across geographies, governance models that survive organisational change, and an operating model that can absorb innovation without disruption.

SAP SuccessFactors plays a central role in this shift, but senior HR leaders know that technology alone does not create capability. What matters just as much is how decisions are made, how trade‑offs are managed, and how intent is preserved as complexity grows.

Once HR technology becomes a strategic enabler rather than a delivery concern, leadership attention naturally shifts toward the areas where coherence is hardest to maintain over time. The visual below reflects three priorities that senior HR leaders consistently return to - not because they are new, but because they are the hardest to sustain as complexity, scale, and ambition increase.

What Max Success Plan provides is not a different set of goals, but a way to operationalise these priorities continuously: through governance, decision support, and expert safeguarding as the organisation evolves.

Harmonising Workforce Data and People Processes

For senior leaders, workforce data is no longer an operational output; it is a strategic asset. It underpins business planning, regulatory confidence, workforce decisions, and executive credibility. When data is inconsistent or fragmented, leaders feel the impact directly: through slower decisions, reduced confidence in analytics, and increased risk exposure.

In complex organisations, data fragmentation is rarely the result of poor intent. It emerges organically from legitimate local optimisation, phased rollouts, acquisitions, regional requirements, and evolving processes. Over time, however, these variations accumulate, and the organisation loses a shared understanding of what the data represents and how it should be used.

Max Success Plan addresses this challenge by safeguarding transformation design, architecture governance, and data and analytics adoption across the lifecycle. The focus is not on enforcing uniformity, but on ensuring that variation is intentional, explainable, and governable. This allows leaders to maintain a trusted data foundation, one they can rely on not just for reporting, but for strategic decisions that affect the entire workforce.

Enhancing HR Operations with AI - Responsibly

You already know this - AI has introduced a new level of urgency into HR leadership conversations. Senior HR leaders are expected to leverage AI to improve efficiency, insight, and employee experience, while simultaneously ensuring ethical use, transparency, compliance, and organisational readiness.

From a leadership perspective, the challenge is not access to AI capabilities. It is knowing when and how to deploy them responsibly at scale. Architectural dependencies, data readiness, governance models, and change maturity all determine whether AI becomes a strategic accelerator, or a source of unmanaged risk.

Max Success Plan supports leaders by combining architecture transformation, continuous innovation guidance, and business transformation management. This enables organisations to move beyond isolated experimentation and towards structured, value‑driven AI adoption. The result is not slower innovation, but innovation that enhances HR operations without undermining trust or stability.

Enabling Informed Workforce Decisions at Scale

As HR becomes more analytical, senior leaders are judged less on insight availability and more on decision quality. Having dashboards and data is no longer enough; leaders need confidence that insights are consistent, comparable, and actionable across the organisation.

This is particularly challenging in environments where systems, teams, and priorities continue to change. Without ongoing alignment, analytics frameworks drift, definitions diverge, and insights lose their strategic value.

Max Success Plan supports this priority by providing continuous evolution guidance and transformation safeguarding. This ensures that analytics adoption, decision frameworks, and governance remain aligned with the organisation’s operating model as it evolves. For leaders, this means they can act with confidence, even as complexity increases, knowing that the foundations supporting their decisions are being actively maintained.

Taken together, these priorities - trusted workforce data, responsible AI adoption, and decision‑quality at scale - point to something broader than operational excellence. They describe a leadership responsibility: ensuring that HR transformation remains aligned with enterprise ambition as complexity, pace, and expectation continue to increase.

This is where Max Success Plan moves beyond delivery support and becomes a distinct leadership instrument.

Why Max Is a Leadership Instrument, Not an IT Upgrade

At executive level, what distinguishes Max Success Plan is not the breadth of services it includes, but the nature of the questions it is designed to support.

Max exists to safeguard strategic intent across time, scale, and organisational change. It provides expert oversight at moments where decisions have long‑term implications: choices around architecture, data models, integration strategy, governance structures, extensibility, and AI adoption - often well before the consequences of those decisions become visible.

For senior HR leaders, this creates a different operating dynamic: instead of intervening reactively to correct drift or resolve misalignment after the fact, leaders gain confidence that the transformation is being continuously interpreted and aligned to strategic objectives as it evolves. The emphasis shifts from course‑correction to deliberate steering.

How Leaders Experience the Difference

Leaders who operate within a Max Success Plan engagement often describe a noticeable shift in where their time and attention are spent: there is less need to arbitrate architectural or governance debates once decisions are already embedded. Less effort is required to reconcile divergent interpretations of earlier design choices. Instead, conversations move earlier in the decision cycle and focus increasingly on future capability, organisational readiness, and opportunity.

This change is enabled by the presence of an independent, experienced layer of oversight whose sole responsibility is to safeguard the transformation as a whole, rather than individual initiatives, teams, or partners. That independence allows guidance to remain anchored to long‑term intent, even as priorities, stakeholders, and timelines change.

For leadership, this matters because it restores confidence in the transformation itself, not just in its delivery.

The Max Success Plan conversation typically begins when complexity is no longer something the organisation simply manages, but something that begins to shape leadership risk. This often occurs when SAP SuccessFactors underpins strategic workforce initiatives, when AI and analytics influence executive‑level decisions, or when the organisation’s ability to adapt becomes visibly linked to the coherence and credibility of its HR landscape.

At that point, the central question is no longer whether SAP SuccessFactors can support the organisation. It is whether the transformation around it is being actively guided with the same level of care and intent as the business strategy it enables. That is the role Max Success Plan is designed to play.

The next step is not a technical review, but a conversation with your Customer Success Manager or SAP Account Executive about whether your organisation would benefit from that level of partnership as it continues to evolve.

entry.title: Re: Response Header validation Error in SAP CPI
entry.content: Please ask the API provider to correct or remove the header by ensuring it does not contain leading spaces, tabs, newlines, or multiline values, and that it follows RFC compliant formatting.

entry.title: Re: Crystal Reports PDF bookmark displays incorrect Japanese character (名 → 吊)
entry.content:
Thank you for the suggestion.

We have considered validating the behavior on a newer version.
However, in our environment, performing a verification with CR 2025
is currently not feasible due to practical constraints.

Specifically:
- Installing CR 2025 is restricted in managed environments
due to security and software whitelisting policies.
- Attempting verification on an isolated machine would require
a product/license code, which is not available for evaluation
at the Community level.

In addition, upgrading to a newer major version cannot be adopted
as a solution in our case due to compatibility and operational constraints.

Given this situation, we would appreciate clarification based on
the current version in use:
- whether this behavior is a known limitation or defect, and
- whether there is any recommended workaround or configuration
for runtime-generated PDF bookmarks.

Thank you for your understanding.

entry.title: Automating MPL and Payload Retrieval in SAP Integration Suite for Enhanced Monitoring
entry.content:
Why we build this solution,

The primary objective is to design a centralized and automated Monitoring solution capable of dynamically retrieving MPL Logs, extracting payload attachment and provide a reusable framework for alerting through Mails or other channel for Integration support activities.

If there is a specific requirement to identify the failed interfaces with Status Message, its Payloads with the exact error information across the Multiple packages within in the required Date and Time range. That required navigation through several monitoring. Now we can achieve this without going to the Monitoring page.

Introduction

Monitoring and troubleshooting integration flows in SAP Integration Suite can become complex when dealing with multiple packages and interfaces. Accessing Message processing Logs (MPL) and their Payload attachments is essential for debugging, Auditing, and Reporting.

This Blog demonstrates a fully Automated Approach to:

  • Fetch the MPL logs dynamically
  • Filter logs based on Time range and integration flow Name 
  • Retrieve payload attachments
  • Process and store Payload data

Step 1: Create OAuth2 client Credentials

Navigate to Monitor→ Integration and APIs→ Manage Security→ Security Material 

create a new credential of type OAuth 2 client credentials
Maintain:

  • Client Id
  • Client secret
  • Token Service URL

OAuth2 client credentials to securely connect to the SAP APIs.

Step 2: Implementation in Sap Integration Suite

Design an Integration Flow by using a Timer Trigger and then configure all the necessary content Modifier, this is where we need to set the control filtering Parameters for MPL Logs. 

Step 3: configure Http Receiver (MPL ODATA  API)

Configure HTTP Adapter:

  • Method:  GET
  • URL:
  • https://<tenant>/api/V1/MessageProcessingLogs 
  • Headers: Accept: application/Json

Step 4: Extract Message GUID

Parse the MPL response which we get from the O DATA API Call and extract the Message GUID (MPL_ID) for the further processing.

In the similar way we can configure the HTTP Adapter to call ODATA APIs to retrieve the attachments and its corresponding value. 

During the Implementation we were also able to extract the Failed Interfaces error Information for specific MPL ID and send the error logs for Monitoring.  Can be used for Error handling in the later stage of development.

 

Challenges faced during Implementation

One of the key challenges during the implementation was to handle the dynamic retrieval of payload attachment using SAP Integration OData APIs. Since the attachments, Metadata and the Payloads are retrieved through multiple APIs calls, designing the flow dynamically without hardcoded package name required careful handling of the MPL properties.

Another challenge we came across was managing the Namespace handling and the Splitter configuration while processing the XML response from APIs. Proper XPath handling and dynamically routing logic were essential to ensure the reliable payload extraction. And throughout the Designing and Execution of this particular Integration flow I have gained a deeper Learning and insight into SAP Integration suite Monitoring APIs and Dynamic I flow filtering.

 

Conclusion:

This Approach demonstrate how SAP Integration Suite Monitoring capabilities can be significantly Enhanced using automated MPL and payload retrieving mechanism to build a Robust monitoring solution in SAP Integration Suite, this can be further Enhanced to 

  • Integrate with Monitoring Dashboards
  • Automated Alert Mechanism detection
  • Dynamically retrieves MPL logs
  • Fetches payload Attachments
  • Supports audit and Analytics

This approach significantly improves visibility, automation, reduces downtime, and enhance operational efficiency in enterprise integrations.

entry.title: Re: Customer Vendor Integration Analysis out of sync in R...
entry.content:
The Readiness Check tile pulls data from the CVI consistency check and compares it with what was uploaded to SAP. Even if you have implemented the latest notes and re-run the report, the tile can still show “Out of Sync” if the uploaded snapshot doesn’t perfectly match the current state of your system. This is especially true after corrections. The Readiness Check report does not always refresh in real time with the CVI-PRECHK results.

What really matters is that the authoritative check is the in-system CVI-PRECHK and CVI Cockpit (CGVI-PRECHK). If both of these come back clean (no errors, no inconsistencies), then your system is technically ready for CVI. The “Out of Sync” in the Readiness Check tile is informational and it is flagging a mismatch between the uploaded report and the current system state, not an actual CVI error.

entry.title: Re: Unable to export cost component split in Display Actu...
entry.content: Yes, the error is due to system limits in the Public Cloud apps. The recommended workaround is to use CDS views instead of the “Display Actual Costing” app for downloading cost component splits. The key CDS views are I_CostComponentSplit (for standard cost estimates) and I_ActualCostComponentSplit (for actual costing). These can be accessed via the Customer Data Browser app

entry.title: SAP MDG + Azure AD - Part 2: Connecting SAP to Azure Active Directory – The Technical Setup
entry.content:
Hi Peers, 

In Part 1, I covered the business case why SAP MDG needs Azure Active Directory and the five use cases it enables. If you haven't read that, I'd recommend starting there for context. Below is the link for Part 1.

SAP MDG + Azure AD - Part 1: The Why, The Gap, and... - SAP Community

In this post, I'll walk you through the complete setup Azure account, app registration, client secrets, API permissions, RFC destinations, SSL certificates, configuration tables, and finally running the program that fetches Azure AD user data into SAP.

Here's the flow in technical terms. SAP sends an HTTP POST to Azure AD's token endpoint with the Client ID and Client Secret. Azure validates these credentials and returns a JWT bearer token. SAP then sends an HTTP GET to the Microsoft Graph API /users endpoint with that token in the Authorization header. Graph API validates the token, checks the app's permissions, and returns user data as JSON. SAP deserializes the JSON into an ABAP internal table.

Two HTTP calls. Two RFC destinations. Three config tables. That's the architecture.

Before jumping in What helped me get through this was the ABAP SDK for Azure repository on GitHub and the work its contributors have put into the documentation and demo programs. Solid resource , I’d recommend you to have a look into it if you need any further clarification.

Now Lets split this technical Deep dive into two parts based on Azure configuration and SAP configuration

Part A: Azure Side Configuration

Step 1: Set Up Your Azure Account and Tenant

Sign up for Azure and follow the below steps to set up a new Tenant, Once logged in, go to All Services → Microsoft Entra ID → Manage Tenant → Create a new Tenant. Note down the Tenant ID you will need this later when configuring the RFC destination in SAP.

Step 2: Register an Application

Now let us Register and Application, this application you register here represents your SAP system in Azure. It gives SAP an identity to authenticate with Azure services.

For this Go to App Registrations → New Registration → Enter a name for your application (e.g., ABAPSDK-DEMO-APP) → Select "Single tenant only" → Click Register.

Step 3: Note the Key Values

After registration, the Overview page displays two values you need to save:

Application ID : Unique ID used to identify our application in ABAP Program

Directory (Tenant ID): This Unique ID represents our organization and this will be used in SM59 as a prefix for the path to identify our Tenant with SAP

Copy both and keep them safe. You will use them throughout the SAP configuration.

Step 4: Generate a Client Secret

Under the registered application, go to Certificates & Secrets → Create a new Client Secret → Set an expiration date → Click Add.

Azure will display two columns: Value and Secret ID. Copy the Value immediately. Value is basically an API key, This is your actual client secret it goes into the ZADF_CONFIG table in SAP.

Important: Azure shows the Value only once. If you navigate away from this page without copying it, you will not be able to retrieve it again. You would have to delete the secret and create a new one.

Step 5: Configure API Permissions

Go to API Permissions → Add a Permission → Select Microsoft Graph → Select Application permissions (not Delegated) → Search for User.Read.All → Add it.

Note: While Creating a new API permission make sure to go with Application permissions are required because SAP uses the OAuth2 client credentials flow which is a server-to-server call with no interactive user sign-in.

Step 6: Grant Admin Consent

After adding the permission, click "Grant admin consent for <your tenant name>" → Confirm by clicking Yes.

Your tenant name in the sense is the new created tenant in our first Step

Verify that the Status column should show a green checkmark with "Granted for <your tenant name>" next to each permission. If the button is greyed out or shows a different tenant name, you are not an administrator of that tenant.

With this, the Azure side configuration is complete.

 

Part B: SAP Side Configuration

Initial Step: Install the ABAP SDK for Azure

The ABAP SDK provides the classes, interfaces, configuration tables, and demo programs needed to communicate with Azure. Refer to the official installation guide on GitHub: https://github.com/Microsoft/ABAP-SDK-for-Azure

Step 1: Create the RFC Destination for Azure AD

Open transaction SM59 and create a new RFC destination of connection type G (HTTP Connection to External Server).

Step 2: Configure Technical Settings

Now configure Target Host, service Port and Path prefix with the following values:

login.microsoftonline.com, 443, /<YOUR_TENANT_ID>/oauth2/token

Replace <YOUR_TENANT_ID> with the Tenant ID you noted in Step 3 of the Azure configuration. Make sure there is a forward slash at the beginning of the path prefix.

Step 3: Configure Logon & Security

Now navigate to Logon & Security Tab under it configure the following, SSL->Active (select the radio button) and SSL Certificate       DFAULT SSL Client (Standard)

Save the destination. Do not test the connection yet it will fail until the SSL certificates are imported.

Step 4: Download SSL Certificates

SAP needs to trust the SSL certificate from login.microsoftonline.com to establish a secure HTTPS connection. Without this, SAP will throw a SSSLERR_PEER_CERT_UNTRUSTED error.

To download the certificates, open Chrome and navigate to: https://login.microsoftonline.com/<YOUR_TENANT_ID>/oauth2/token

You will see an error page saying "The endpoint only accepts POST, OPTIONS requests." This is normal the token endpoint only accepts POST requests. You are here only to download the certificate.

Click the lock icon next to the URL → View Certificate → Go to the Certification Path tab → Export every certificate in the chain

Warning: You must export ALL certificates in the chain not just one. If you miss the root or intermediate certificate, the SSL handshake will still fail.

Step 5: Import Certificates into STRUST

Contact your Basis team and share the downloaded certificates to STRUST and restart ICM

The "Add to Certificate List" step is critical importing alone loads the certificate but does not add it to the trust store. Without this step, the handshake will still fail.

Step 6: Test the RFC Connection

After the certificates are imported and the ICM is restarted, go back to SM59 and test the connection. You should see HTTP 200 OK. If you still get the SSL error, verify that all certificates in the chain were imported and added to the certificate list.

Step 7: Configure the ABAP SDK Tables

The ABAP SDK uses three configuration tables. Each Azure service interface requires entries

ZREST_CONFIG - Interface to RFC Mapping

This is the master table that links an Interface ID to an RFC destination.

Field

Value

Interface ID

DEMO_AZ_AD

RFC Destination

AZURE_ABAPSDKKDEMO-AAD (your SM59 destination)

ZREST_CONF_MISC - Operational Settings

This table controls the HTTP method, retry logic, and alert settings.

Field

Value

Interface ID

DEMO_AZ_AD

METHOD

POST (the OAuth token endpoint only accepts POST)

MAX_RETRY

1

EMAIL_ID

Your email address (for failure alerts)

MAIL_BODY_TXT

Error text identifier

RETRY_METHOD

Leave blank

ZADF_CONFIG — Azure-Specific Configuration

This table holds the authentication type, client secret, and processing mode.

Field

Value

Interface ID

DEMO_AZ_AD

Interface Type

Azure Active Directory (select from dropdown)

SAS_KEY

Paste your client secret VALUE from Azure Step 4

URI

Leave blank

SERVICE_TYPE

S (Synchronous)

IS_TRY

Leave blank

This table holds the authentication type, client secret, and processing mode.

Important: The Interface ID must be exactly the same across all three tables. It is case-sensitive.

Important: The Interface Type in ZADF_CONFIG determines which ABAP class the SDK factory creates. For AAD token generation, it must be "Azure Active Directory". Setting it to anything else will result in a MOVE_CAST_ERROR at runtime.

With this, the connection between Azure Active Directory and your SAP system is established.

Now let us configure one more RFC destination To fetch data from Azure, we need API access to Microsoft Graph. This requires a separate RFC destination for the Graph API endpoint.

Step 8: Create the RFC Destination for Graph API

Open SM59 and create a new destination:

Step 9: Configure Logon & Security

Follow the same SSL certificate process to download the certificate chain from https://graph.microsoft.com, import into STRUST, restart ICM, and test the connection. You should get HTTP 200.

Step 10: Configure the ABAP SDK Tables for Graph API

now Create entries in all three tables with a new Interface ID:

ZREST_CONFIG

Field

Value

Interface ID

AZ_GRAPH

RFC Destination

AZURE_GRAPH

ZREST_CONF_MISC

Field

Value

Interface ID

AZ_GRAPH

METHOD

GET

MAX_RETRY

1

ZADF_CONFIG

Field

Value

Interface ID

AZ_GRAPH

Interface Type

Microsoft Graph (select from dropdown)

SAS_KEY

Same client secret VALUE as DEMO_AZ_AD

URI

Leave blank

SERVICE_TYPE

S

IS_TRY

Leave blank

Important: The Interface Type for the Graph API interface must be "Microsoft Graph" not "Azure Active Directory". The factory class uses this value to instantiate the correct service class. Wrong type means wrong class, which means a MOVE_CAST_ERROR.

With this, the connection between Microsoft Graph API and your SAP system is established.

Now let us test the connection with The ABAP SDKs existing program this includes a demo program called ZADF_DEMO_AZURE_GRAPH that can fetch users from Azure AD.

Step 11: Open and Update the Program

Go to transaction SE38 and open program ZADF_DEMO_AZURE_GRAPH. Update the Interface ID constants at the top of the program to match your configuration:

Step 12: Execute

Run the program (F8). On the selection screen:

Field

Value

Select

"Read Users" radio button

Azure Client ID

Your Application (Client) ID from Azure Step 3

Step 13: Review the Output

The program will request an AAD token from login.microsoftonline.com, call the Microsoft Graph API /users endpoint with that token, and display the user data in JSON format.

If the program returns user data, your end-to-end integration is working. SAP is authenticating with Azure AD, calling Microsoft Graph API, and receiving data all of this from ABAP.

 

 

What's Next

That's the complete technical setup of Azure side and SAP side. Two RFC destinations, three config tables each, and a working demo program pulling live user data from Azure Active Directory into SAP.

But fetching data through a demo program is just the foundation. In Part 3, I'll show you how to take this further by building a reusable ABAP class that wraps the entire Azure authentication and Graph API flow into a clean interface that can be consumed in to FPM UI in MDG Business Partner screen. That's where this moves from a technical demo to a practical MDG solution.

Stay tuned.

entry.title: Configure cluster environment using SUSE Pacemaker for HANA replication
entry.content:
In manual HANA replication configuration.We have to perform Take over/switch over manually.To automate take over/switch over.we have to configure cluster environment using SUSE Pacemaker.

 

Below are the steps
1.NFSSRV2-NFS server
2.HANADBPRI-Primary HANA DB
3.HANADBHA-Secondary/HA HANA DB
4.HANADBVHOST-Virtual IP/Floating IP(It will be moving here based on the active nodes.
Note:Virtual IP once added will not be reachable until Pacemaker is confifgured

Pre-Requisites
1.Install HANA DB
2.Configure replication
3.Below OS packages needs to be installed
zypper install -t pattern ha_sles-Mandatory
zypper in corosync-qdevice- Not Mandatory.Required only id Q devices are installed
zypper in SAPHanaSR-Mandatory
zypper in corosync-qnetd-Not Mandatory.Required only id Q devices are installed
4.Install Primary and Secondary/HA DB with Local host name.

Note:In cluster environment we install HANA DB with local hostname and not with Virtual host name.Whereas Cluster environment with other products like ASCS,ERS we will be install SAP using Virtual Host Name

 

We are using setup as below

 

Scenario 1:

Suse pacemaker will be sending heartbeat from Primary to Secondary/HA DB(Basically it is called as Ping).If we are not getting the reply for the ping.Then Pacemaker will bring up Secondary/HA DB

Scenario 2:

Split Brain scenario-Both Primary and Secondary/HA DB’s will be alive but due to some network issue replication will be broken and no heart beat signal will be passed to each other.Primary DB and Secondary/HA DB thinks that each other DB is down & dead(But both DB will be up) and try to pull up the cluster and bring the resources(Virtual IP/Floating IP) in their respective DB thinking other DB is down.This causes confusion to Suse pacemaker and network.This scenario is called as Split Brain Scenario.


To Avoid the Split Brain Scenario we have 2 solutions


1.SDB-1 shared disk is created in the virtualized platforms and it is mounted in both the platforms and status of the cluster is stored in the SDB and it very small in size almost 1mb.If SDB is disconnected then it the DB will think that network is down.If SDB is available and Hana DB is not available.Then it will think that Hana DB is down and take over/switch over will happen.


2.Q device using NFS Filesystem
Note:When the DB is down the only virtual/Floating IP will be shifted from primary to secondary/HA DB because replication is active and Data will be present in secondary.Where in SAP ASCS/ERS will be installed and complete Filesystem will be moved from Primary to HA server.
Configure replication between Primary & Secondary/HA DB.Take backup for primary and Secondary/HA DB and enable replication.

 

 

Note:
1.Scale Up-Only 1 systemwe can increase the Ram and other resources.
2.Scale Out-We can add the multiple nodes(Mater node->Worker Node->Standy node)

 

Configuration of Cluster

1.This command will work only when the above mentioned packages are installed

 

2.Ring is interface for network communication. It will be created in both HA and DR.It will automatically take up the local IP

 

3.We are following the Qdevice using NFS Server method

 

 

We are going with the Q device method
Note:The below highlighted packages mandatorily needs to be installed in the NFS Server to Run Q devices.

 

 

Current status if cluster-crm status

 

To check the live changes in cluster-crm_mon

 

Check whether watchdog is active or not.It is used to check the services running on OS level

 

Now cluster is initiated in Primary Node.Cluster is the one time activity which needs to be configured only once on 1 node and in another node we can join the existing cluster
Note:Cluster join means any cluster configuration changes made in one server will reflect into another server

 

Check whether watchdog is running or not

 

Watchdog is not running,or else it will show the line as dog
Now start the watchdog
If watchdog is not active create it
echo softdog > /etc/modules-load.d/watchdog.conf
start watchdog
systemctl restart systemd-modules-load.service

 

Put cluster into maintainence mode

 

We need to add hana related resources into the server.It can be added from any node.We need to add the resource agent(RA).RA is the utility used to perform or monitor the operations.


There are 2 resource agent

1.SAP HANA topology(Used to check whether hana services is crashed or not)

2.SAP HANA(Used to start and stop the database) are the two RA.
Note:We can add primitives in either primary or secondary server node.It will automatically sync to another server.But in this scenario I am adding primitive in 1st server.

 

Add the resource agent

 

Add the 1st primitive
primitive rsc_SAPHanaTop_HDB_HDB00 ocf:suse:SAPHanaTopology \ #Monitor the HANA services#
op monitor interval="10" timeout="600" \ #Will monitor the service for every 10 secs and should get an reply within 600 sec#
op start interval="0" timeout="600" \#Hana DB stop and start time#
op stop interval="0" timeout="300" \\#Hana DB stop and start time#
params SID="HDB" InstanceNumber="00"#HANA DB SID and instance number#
clone cln_SAPHanaTop_HDB_HDB00 rsc_SAPHanaTop_HDB_HDB00 \ #Clone of the HANA topology#

 

Once saved clone is set into secondary

 

SAP HANA Topology resource agent comes with the package zypper SAP HANA SR

 

Add 2nd primitive HANA DB Resource Agent for HANA db start and stop in primary server

 

primitive rsc_SAPHana_HDB_HDB00 ocf:suse:SAPHana \
op start interval="0" timeout="3600" \
op stop interval="0" timeout="3600" \
op promote interval="0" timeout="3600" \
op monitor interval="60" role="Master" timeout="700" \
op monitor interval="61" role="Slave" timeout="700" \
params SID="HDB" InstanceNumber="00" PREFER_SITE_TAKEOVER="true" \
DUPLICATE_PRIMARY_TIMEOUT="7200" AUTOMATED_REGISTER="true" \
meta priority="100"
ms msl_SAPHana_HDB_HDB00 rsc_SAPHana_HDB_HDB00 \
meta clone-max="2" clone-node-max="1" interleave="true"

 

 

save

 

Add constraints into the primary server

Two constraints are organizing the correct placement of the virtual IP address for the client database access and the start order between the two resource agents SAPHana and SAPHanaTopology.

entry.title: Re: Toerance Error while budget Transfer
entry.content:
Why you see “Tolerance limit of 100% for WBS element W-006-010 has been reached” is because, WBS W-006-010 is part of the same project hierarchy and has AVC activated. The tolerance limit for that WBS (or its controlling node) is set at 100%. When you transfer budget from W-006-001 to W-006-002, the system re‑evaluates the overall budget situation and finds that W-006-010 is already at or beyond its allowed consumption. Even though you are not directly transferring to W-006-010, the AVC check is performed at the project definition or higher-level WBS node, so the error is triggered.

Perform the below steps

1) Check AVC Profile
Transaction OPI1 (Availability Control Settings) or via SSCUI in Public Cloud. Verify the tolerance limits (e.g., 100% warning, 105% error). If the error is set at 100%, any full consumption blocks further transfers.

2) Review Budget Distribution
Use CJ30 (Change Project Budget) or the Public Cloud equivalent app to check how budget is distributed across WBS elements. Ensure W-006-010 has sufficient budget or adjust its allocation.

3) Adjust Tolerance Limits
If business allows, increase the tolerance limit (e.g., error at 105% instead of 100%). This gives flexibility for transfers without blocking.

4)Transfer Budget at Higher Level
Sometimes transfers must be done at the grouping WBS or project definition level rather than directly between lower-level WBS elements, depending on how AVC is configured.

5) Simulation Run
Use CJBN (Budget Availability Control Simulation) to see which WBS elements are causing the block before executing the transfer.

So in short, the error is not about the two WBS elements you are transferring between, but about another WBS (W-006-010) in the same project hierarchy hitting its tolerance limit. Fix by either redistributing budget to W-006-010 or adjusting the AVC tolerance settings.

entry.title: Re: Onboarding 2.0 - External to Internal Hires Behavior
entry.content:
Hello @angelosuano ,

ConvertExternalUserOnStartDateJob will only convert the candidate to an active employee if the hire status of the candidate is hired and the process status is open within the Process Object associated with the extenal user.
If the candidate is still in future date, the candidate can edit his/her employee details.

  • Before the completion of Manage Pending Hire, the candidate can edit the details.
  • After the completion of Manage Pending Hire, the admin user can edit the employee details until the start date. 

As an external user, the candidates will have access to the system until the start date. However, they will only be confined to viewing the information present on the homepage and Onboarding Checklist. 
After the conversion to an active employee, they will receive the necessary information to login as an active employee allowing a wider access to SF modules.

entry.title: Exploring a Desktop Companion for SAP Integration Suite Development
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

Over the last few years working with SAP Integration Suite landscapes, I kept noticing the same pattern during day-to-day integration work: a lot of time is spent switching contexts between tenants, reviewing artifacts manually, documenting integrations, troubleshooting monitor messages, and preparing deployments across environments.

Especially in landscapes with DEV, QA, and PROD tenants, even relatively simple tasks can become repetitive and fragmented. Comparing iFlows between systems, checking whether artifacts are aligned, reviewing failed messages, or generating technical documentation often involves multiple browser tabs, monitor screens, exported files, and manual notes.

Because of that, I started building a local desktop application called SAP IS Companion as a personal side project to explore whether some of these repeated workflows could be simplified into a more focused developer workspace.

The goal is not to replace SAP Integration Suite itself, but to provide a companion-style workbench for inspection, comparison, documentation, migration preparation, monitoring, and developer support tasks around Integration Suite development.

Tenant Comparison and Landscape Visibility

One of the most common pain points I encountered was comparing content across tenants.

When working across DEV, QA, and PROD environments, it can become difficult to quickly identify:

  • Missing packages
  • Missing iFlows
  • Version differences
  • Deployment inconsistencies
  • Landscape drift over time

Today, many teams still rely on manual checks or exported artifacts to validate tenant alignment.

The comparison workflow in SAP IS Companion focuses on helping visualize those differences between tenants in a simpler way before deployments or migration activities occur.

iFlow Documentation Support

Another area that frequently consumes time is documentation.

Even when integrations are implemented correctly, maintaining updated technical documentation for iFlows can become difficult during active projects or support activities.

One area I’m experimenting with is generating draft documentation from iFlow metadata and structure to help developers and support teams review integrations faster and reduce some of the manual documentation effort.

The idea is not to replace technical review, but to help create a stronger starting point for documentation and knowledge sharing.

Failed Message Review and Incident Context

Monitor investigations are another workflow that can become repetitive, especially during support periods or production incidents.

When reviewing failed messages, developers often need to manually gather:

  • Error details
  • Exception information
  • Message context
  • Monitor references
  • Payload details
  • Suggested troubleshooting notes

I started experimenting with generating clearer incident-style summaries from monitor information to make troubleshooting discussions and handoffs easier between teams.

Developer Helper Tools

I also wanted to explore smaller utilities that could help with common integration-development tasks.

Some examples include:

  • Generating XSDs from payload samples
  • Draft XPath and JSONPath expressions
  • Router condition helpers
  • Content Modifier snippet generation
  • Mapping starter structures
  • Payload inspection helpers

These are relatively small workflows individually, but they appear frequently during iFlow development and troubleshooting.

Authentication and Security Considerations

One of the most important areas I’m thinking through carefully is authentication and enterprise readiness.

Since the application connects to SAP Integration Suite tenants using tenant API credentials, topics such as:

  • credential storage
  • local encryption
  • API scope restrictions
  • least-privilege access
  • auditability
  • enterprise authentication patterns

become extremely important.

Right now, I’m continuing to evaluate safer and more enterprise-friendly approaches around credential handling and tenant access.

I would especially be interested in hearing how others would expect authentication to work in a desktop companion-style application for Integration Suite.

Questions for the Community

I’d be very interested to hear feedback from SAP Integration Suite developers, consultants, and BASIS/admin teams:

  • Which Integration Suite workflows are currently the most repetitive or frustrating?
  • How are you currently comparing artifacts between tenants?
  • Do your teams maintain iFlow documentation manually today?
  • Would generated failed-message summaries or incident-style documentation be useful?
  • Are there other helper tools you wish existed around message mapping, payload analysis, or monitoring?
  • What authentication or security expectations would you have for a desktop Integration Suite companion application?

Conclusion

Building SAP IS Companion started mainly as an attempt to simplify workflows I personally kept running into while working with Integration Suite projects and tenant landscapes.

What stood out to me most during development was how much integration work can lose time in context switching between monitoring, documentation, comparison, and migration-related tasks.

The project is still evolving, and security and authentication remain some of the most important areas I’m continuing to evaluate carefully.

More than anything, I’m interested in hearing how others in the SAP Integration Suite space are currently handling these challenges and whether there are workflows that could benefit from better tooling or developer-focused utilities.

Disclosure: SAP IS Companion is an independent side project and is not affiliated with or endorsed by SAP.

Additional disclosure: AI tools were used to help refine and rewrite portions of this blog post for clarity and structure.

entry.title: This has been one of those weeks where you feel like a lucky person by Prof. Rafael

[This is the translation of the original LinkedIn post in Spanish, which you can find here: Post | Feed | LinkedIn] 

If we had an incredibly good time on Tuesday at the #SAPBusinessExperience, yesterday we were lucky enough to have the good work of Andrea Ollo and Katrin Simon again in a meeting with teachers from the Universitat Politècnica de València (UPV) to discover the benefits of #ERPsim in the classroom and test its potential as a gamification strategy for undergraduate and master's subjects related to business management.

Andrea shared with us her experience with the simulator in different learning situations, something very valuable that we would never find in the simulator manual, and that can only come from teams like those of the Universidad Pública de Navarra that have been working intensively on it. And of course, there was a test, with a couple of rounds selling breakfast cereals, buying strawberries and blueberries, and making boxes. And how engaging the game is!

There has been a recent enhancements with this regard. You can now directly restart the i-9 process from the onboarding dashboard itself. We have the option to "Initiate I-9 Process" via the Actions menu in the candidate details page. This option will allow you to trigger correction for i-9 section 1, i-9 section 2 and reverification.

For more information, you can refer here: Initiate the U.S Form I-9 Correction or Reverification Processes from the New Hire Details Page 

Similarly, you can also cancel the existing I-9 from the onboarding dashboard through new hire details page. 
For more information, you can refer here: Cancel Form I-9 Process from the New Hire Details page 

@Melissa_S "Correct My Personal Data" option during i-9 section 1 can be used by new hire themselves to correct any incorrect information which is displayed in the form i-9 section 1. 
Initiate i-9 Process and Trigger I-9 flow are options given to the admin user to trigger the correction if the candidate has missed the opportunity to use "Correct My Personal Data" or if the candidate is already converted to an employee.

entry.title: Receiver Kafka Adapter Scenario with AVRO Serialization and Schema Registry in SAP Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Cloud Integration (CI) provides a standard Kafka receiver adapter that allows integration flows to publish messages to Kafka topics. However, the adapter currently does not support AVRO serialization or Schema Registry integration natively. This limitation is already acknowledged by SAP and is listed as a long-term improvement item on the SAP Influence portal.

In one of our integration scenarios, we had a requirement to publish messages in Confluent AVRO wire format, with schemas managed centrally in a Schema Registry. Since the standard adapter does not yet support this, we implemented a custom solution using Groovy scripting and external libraries.

This blog post focuses on:

  • Working with Schema Registry
  • Handling AVRO serialization
  • Preparing messages for the Kafka receiver adapter

ℹ️ Basic Kafka adapter configuration and CI fundamentals are not covered here, as they are already well documented in SAP Help and SAP Integration Community.

 

Schema Registry

Kafka treats messages as raw byte arrays and does not enforce any structure on the data. This can easily lead to incompatibilities between producers and consumers.

A Schema Registry solves this problem by:

  • Acting as a central repository for message schemas
  • Assigning a unique schema ID to each schema version
  • Ensures both producers and consumers agree on the message structure

For a Kafka producer using Confluent AVRO format, the message must contain:

  • A schema ID
  • The payload serialized according to the corresponding AVRO schema

Therefore, before publishing a message to Kafka, the producer must know:

  1. The schema ID
  2. The schema definition (schema value)

 

Approaches to Retrieve Schema Information

First step is to get the Schema details for the AVRO conversion. We explored two possible options to retrieve the schema ID and schema value inside Cloud Integration capability of SAP Integration Suite.

Option 1: Calling the Schema Registry API

In this approach, the integration flow makes a HTTP call to the Schema Registry REST API to fetch the latest schema ID and schema value dynamically.

HTTP call to Schema Registry

Steps involved
  • Call the Schema Registry endpoint
  • Extract the schema ID and schema value from the response using a Groovy script
  • Store these values in Exchange Properties for later use
Pros
  • Always retrieves the latest schema
  • No manual updates required
Cons
  • Additional HTTP call for every message
  • Slight performance impact and extra cost

This approach is ideal when schemas change frequently or must always stay in sync.

 

Option 2: Storing Schema in Partner Directory (Chosen Approach)

The second approach is to store the schema value statically in the Partner Directory and retrieve it at runtime using a Groovy script. The Schema ID is externalized as a configurable parameter. We opted for this approach because our schemas do not change often.

Schema stored in Partner Directory

Steps involved
  • Store the schema value as a JSON file as Binary Parameters in Partner Directory
  • Extract the schema value in the integration flow using a Groovy script
  • Set the schema id as an externalized Exchange Property in the integration flow
Pros
  • It avoids repeated outbound HTTP calls
  • Improves performance and reduces runtime overhead
Cons
  • Whenever the schema changes in the Schema Registry, the schema value and schema ID in CI must be updated manually
    
//***Step 1 : Get the schema value from Partner Directory***//

    // Instance of Partner Directory Service
    def service = ITApiFactory.getApi(PartnerDirectoryService.class, null); 
    if (service == null){
        throw new IllegalStateException("Partner Directory Service not found");
    }
    
    // get the schema value from the Partner Directory
    def schemaBinary = service.getParameter(streamName, "SchemaRegistry" , BinaryData.class);
    
    // set the schema value to a property if it exists
    if (schemaBinary != null){
        byte[] schemaBytes = schemaBinary.getData()
        schemaValue = new String(schemaBytes, StandardCharsets.UTF_8)
    } else {
        throw new IllegalStateException("Schema not found for stream $streamName in Partner Directory");   
    }

 

Payload Conversion to AVRO Format

Once the schema ID and schema value are available, the next step is to convert the incoming message payload to AVRO format before sending it to Kafka.

Scenario Overview

  • Incoming payload: JSON
  • Target output: Confluent AVRO binary format

The conversion is done using a Groovy script with external libraries.

Inputs to the Groovy Script

The Groovy script requires:

  • Input JSON payload
  • AVRO schema definition
  • Schema ID

Output of the Groovy Script in Confluent Wire Format

The output binary produced by the script follows the Confluent serialization format:

[Magic Byte][Schema ID][Serialized Data]

  • Byte 0 – Magic byte (always 0 for AVRO)
  • Bytes 1–4 – 4‑byte schema ID
  • Remaining bytes – AVRO‑serialized record data

The Kafka receiver adapter publishes this binary data directly to the topic.

External Libraries Used

To achieve the conversion, the following libraries were added as external resources:

  • Apache Avro
  • Jackson (for JSON parsing)
Since this solution relies on external libraries and the AVRO conversion logic can be reused across multiple integration scenarios, it is recommended to implement it as a reusable Script Collection. This approach helps keep individual integration flows lightweight by avoiding repeated library uploads, and it also simplifies maintenance by centralizing the logic in one place.
//***Step 2 : Convert the JSON input to AVRO***//

    ByteArrayOutputStream output = null;
    try {
        def schema = new Schema.Parser().parse(schemaValue);

        // Convert JSON to Avro bytes
        AvroSchema jacksonAvroSchema = new AvroSchema(schema);
        AvroMapper avroMapper = new AvroMapper();
        output = new ByteArrayOutputStream();
        avroMapper.writer(jacksonAvroSchema).writeValue(output, new ObjectMapper().readTree(payload));
        byte[] avroBytes = output.toByteArray();

        // Build Confluent format: [magic byte][schema ID][data]
        ByteBuffer buffer = ByteBuffer.allocate(1 + 4 + avroBytes.length)
        buffer.put((byte) 0)
        buffer.putInt(Integer.parseInt(schemaId.trim()))
        buffer.put(avroBytes)
        buffer.flip()

        // Set header and body
        message.setHeader("kafka.KEY", "")
        message.setBody(buffer.array());

    } catch (Exception e) {
        throw new RuntimeException("Failed to convert JSON to Avro format: " + e.getMessage(), e);
    } finally {
        // Close output stream to prevent resource leaks
        if (output != null) {
            try {
                output.close();
            } catch (Exception e) {
                messageLog.addCustomHeaderProperty("Warning", "ByteArrayOutputStream cannot be closed. " + e.getMessage());
            }
        }
        // Close reader to prevent resource leaks
        if (payload != null) {
            try {
                payload.close();
            } catch (Exception e) {
                messageLog.addCustomHeaderProperty("Warning", "Input Reader cannot be closed. " + e.getMessage());
            }
        }
    }

 

Alternative Approach

It is also possible to implement the solution using only the Apache Avro libraries, without relying on Jackson. However, in our experience, this approach led to challenges when the AVRO schema contained optional fields defined as unions with null (for example, "type": ["null", "string"]).

If your schema does not include union fields, this approach is a better option.

    // Convert JSON to Avro object    
        def reader = new GenericDatumReader<Object>(schema)    
        Decoder decoder = DecoderFactory.get().jsonDecoder(schema, payload)    
        Object datum = reader.read(null, decoder)
       
    // Serialize Avro object to binary   
        def writer = new GenericDatumWriter<Object>(schema)    
        Encoder encoder = EncoderFactory.get().binaryEncoder(output, null)    
        writer.write(datum, encoder)    
        encoder.flush()    
        byte[] avroBytes = output.toByteArray()

 

Conclusion

Until SAP provides native support for AVRO and Schema Registry in the Kafka adapter, this custom approach offers a way to:

  • Work with Schema Registry
  • Serialize messages into Confluent AVRO format
  • Publish compatible messages to Kafka from SAP Cloud Integration 

Finally, I would like to thank Sunny Kapoor for his valuable insights, and support in shaping this blog post.

entry.title: Re: Cost Optimization in SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
Thanks Raghu

I hoped for a little more. To optimize cost is almost always, minimizing cost (if you ask in Finance). I think that it is more relevant to look at how do you optimize performance under some economic restrictions. 

Average is not a good measure except if you have a 24/7 business with same utilization all the time. It is more relevant to look at how many users that are hit by memory restrictions or figure out if you have loads that fail due to too few resources. Also following up on trends in consumption is important to ensure that you don't run into problems in the near future.

With kind regards

Kristian

entry.title: Automatic loading WT without entries in SMQ2
entry.content:
Hi EWM Experts,

We are working in the ASR process. We encounter these SMQ2 entries:

Loading cannot begin until freight order is assigned. /SCWM/SHP_RCV 679
The status of the relevant freight order doesn't allow loading. /SCWM/RF_DE 166

The root cause is a customizing setting for the POSC:

Our outbound process is very simple:

  1. Picking
  2. Packing
  3. Staging
    1. HU is moved to the staging area
    2. Backgroundjob sets shipping readiness
    3. We plan a freight order based on the consignment order in TM
    4. We set the freight order ready for warehouse execution
  4. SMQ2 entry is retriggered and creates loading WT

Step 4 only occurs, if the steps 3.1 - 3.4 are completed.
We require the automatic WT, as we don't want any user to create the loading WT manually.

However, this obviously creates quite some load in SMQ2.


Now my question is: Is there another way to create the WT automatically after the freight order has the correct status?
And additionally: I have in my mind, that our approach is not the SAP recommended; however I fail to find the official SAP recommendation for loading WT creation. Can you point me to the source?

Thank you!

entry.title: MANDATORY TEXT HEADER FIELD AT GROUP JOURNAL ENTRY POST
entry.content:
We would like to implement a validation rule to make mandatory the text field of the header, DocumentItemText, of a group journal entry. It should be validated when the user would post through the following applications:

  • “Post Group Journal Entries” F2971
  • “Import Group Journal Entries” F3073

Our approach is to create a validation rule through "Manage Substitution/Validation Rules – Group Journal Entries" F4407 app, using "GR Journal Entry Item" as business context.

However, when searching for the field in the Validation section of the rule, we are unable to locate the field "DocumentItemText" in the Field/Function selector.

Is “DocumentItemText” the right name of the related text field? Or perhaps is that field missing?

If yes, would you mind to let us know how could it be enabled to make it mandatory?

We have seen that there is  the following BAdIs : FIN_ACDOC_HEADER_VALIDATION - Journal Entry Header Validation 

Is there an equivalent but for Group Reporting Journal entry that would validate if the text field of the header would be filled?

entry.title: Re: Custom controller for a Fiori elements V4 app

entry.title: SAP Joule- The "Totally not an official ITSM connector"
entry.content:
SAP Joule isn’t a magical ITSM plug that sits between systems wearing a badge. It’s more like an enthusiastic translator at a networking event who says, “Oh, you’ve got an incident in ServiceNow? I’ll just whisper that into the API for you.”

So instead of “SAP Joule is an ITSM connector” Think: “SAP Joule intelligent assistant is that clever middle layer in SAP applications like S4HANA, Build Work Zone/Fiori that chats with ServiceNow via APIs, turns your casual complaints into structured incidents, and then politely updates them when new test results arrive—like a very overqualified messenger who never complains about copy-pasting JSON and knows how to get the job done through APIs while you just stand there wondering how it became so competent..”

In short: it’s not officially an ITSM connector , but it fakes it quite efficiently, powered entirely by APIs and good intentions. ( Using SAP Joule Studio in Build and custom Joule skills)

Incident creation from SAP Joule

When users interact with SAP Build Work Zone/Fiori or any SAP Joule enabled application, SAP Joule can act as an embedded AI assistant that interprets user requests, system alerts, or test outcomes. Instead of switching tools, users can simply describe an issue in natural language (for example, “login failure in payroll app after deployment or system dump”), and SAP Joule can:

 

  • Format the problem into a structured ITSM ticket
  • Create a new incident directly in ServiceNow

 

This reduces friction between business users and IT operations teams, ensuring incidents are logged consistently and quickly and with improved quality.

As shown below, SAP Joule asks to provide some mandatory inputs ( can be customized), which can be ingested in one single well drafted prompt, separated by commas and an incident is submitted in service now in Realtime.

Incident successfully created with the right inputs. Some inputs can be made mandatory to control the incident quality and later operational analysis.

Updating incidents with testing results

Beyond creation, SAP Joule can also manage the lifecycle of incidents. As users validate fixes or reproduce issues, they can feed results back into SAP Joule, which then:

  • Updates existing ServiceNow incidents with comments
  • Reflects status changes such as “In Testing,” “Resolved,” or “Reopened” (possible)

For example, after testing, a user can simply instruct SAP Joule to append results to an open incident. The update is immediately reflected in ServiceNow, ensuring real-time synchronization between SAP and ITSM systems.

Advantages of using SAP Joule for incident generation

Integrating SAP Joule with ServiceNow could provide several operational benefits:

  • Faster incident creation: Users can log incidents directly from business workflows without switching platforms
  • Reduced manual effort: Natural language processing eliminates the need for structured form filling
  • Improved accuracy: Context-aware AI reduces incomplete or inconsistent incident descriptions.
  • Seamless integration: Direct connection between SAP applications and ServiceNow improves end-to-end visibility
  • Real-time updates: Test results and comments can be pushed instantly into active incident

Summary

I only discussed two possibilities but the capabilities could go far beyond this.

  • Search incidents or Search known solutions
  • Read knowledge articles in service now (Document grounding with Service Now)
  • Auto assign Category/sub-category based on inputs or past incidents.

By positioning SAP Joule as an intelligent ITSM companion, organizations could bridge SAP-centric business processes with ServiceNow-based incident management. SAP Joule Embedded within SAP applications, could transform how incidents are created, enriched, and resolved—making IT operations more responsive, automated, and closely aligned with business activity.

Note:  GenAI was used for language refinement and tone improvement.

entry.title: SAP and Tecmilenio drive the development of digital talent in Mexico with a co-innovation model
entry.content:
[Check out the official Press Release (PR) in Spanish: SAP y Tecmilenio impulsan la formación de talento digital en México con un modelo de co-innovación - SAP News Center Latinoamérica]

  • Through solutions such as SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP S/4HANA, Tecmilenio exposes more than 4,000 students each year across its 33 campuses to enterprise technology used in the industry.
  • SAP Labs Latin America works with academic institutions in the region to accelerate co-innovation and bridge the gap between university education and real market demand.
  • Nine out of ten Tecmilenio graduates obtain employment, in a context where digital skills have become one of the market’s main requirements.

Monterrey, Nuevo León, April 28, 2026.— SAP Mexico and Tecmilenio maintain a collaboration aimed at accelerating the training of digital talent in the country. The initiative integrates SAP cloud solutions within the institution’s educational model and connects students with technology platforms that already operate in the business environment, with the goal of reducing the gap between university training and the profiles demanded by industry today.

Co-innovation in the cloud to connect the classroom with industry
Tecmilenio, founded in 2002 as part of the Monterrey Institute of Technology ecosystem, operates 33 campuses in Mexico with an educational model oriented toward competencies and links to the productive sector. As a SAP customer, it has incorporated cloud solutions such as SAP SuccessFactors and SAP Concur to support its internal operations. In parallel, within the classroom tools such as SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP S/4HANA are integrated for instructional purposes, enabling students to develop capabilities in analytics, planning, and understanding end-to-end business processes.

This integration enables students to interact with enterprise platforms used in areas such as data analytics, financial planning, and comprehensive management of organizational processes. Through the use of tools such as SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP S/4HANA, the model strengthens data-driven decision-making and understanding of end-to-end processes, shortening the distance between what is learned at university and the demands of real-world operating environments in the industry.

“Digital transformation has changed the talent profile that companies need. At SAP Mexico we collaborate with institutions like Tecmilenio so that students have access to enterprise technology during their training and enter the labor market with competencies already aligned to the real industry demand,” said Dennison John, Managing Director of SAP Labs for Latin America.

SAP Labs Latin America: Technology Developed in the Region

A central component of this collaboration is the role of SAP Labs Latin America, the research and development centers where SAP designs, tests, and scales technology solutions with global reach. From these spaces, SAP promotes co-innovation with customers, academic institutions, and local providers, which facilitates building solutions jointly and transferring industry knowledge to the educational environment.

More than 4,000 students per year access SAP-related content at Tecmilenio’s 33 campuses. This early exposure to enterprise technology has a direct impact on employability: nine out of ten graduates obtain employment, according to institutional data.

The labor market context reinforces the relevance of this bet. The World Economic Forum notes that digital skills, analytical thinking, and lifelong learning will be central requirements for most jobs by the end of the decade. In the Mexican case, this translates into pressure on educational institutions to update their models at the pace of the industry.

The transformation also extends to the teaching staff. Ongoing training of teachers in digital tools, along with updating the curriculum toward flexible, competency-based models, enables teaching to stay in step with the professional environment.

“At Tecmilenio we form people with life purpose, prepared for a constantly changing work environment. The collaboration with SAP allows us to integrate world-class technology in the classroom, broaden our students’ digital competencies, and open early entry opportunities to the industry, in some cases even before graduation,” said Miren Edurne Ballesteros Beascoa, Director of Schools and Programs at Tecmilenio.

There are already cases of students who have joined SAP before completing their studies, which confirms the link between academic training and real industry demand. SAP Mexico and Tecmilenio continue to keep this co-innovation agenda open as a pathway to respond to the country’s digital talent needs in the coming years.

SAP Labs Latin America, through its Academic Partnerships program, works with regional academic institutions to accelerate co-innovation and reduce the gap between university training and the real needs of the labor market. As part of this alliance, Tecmilenio students and faculty will have access to educational programs, tools, and specialized content that strengthen highly demanded skills in the digital and business ecosystem. Under the “empowering students to bring out their best” approach, SAP drives learning, research, and innovation through business applications and technologies that prepare students for professional opportunities in a global environment. More information about the program is available at SAP Academic Alliances.

entry.title: New “Sustainable Finance” Category for SAP Green Ledger on SAP Continuous Influence
entry.content:
We are happy to announce a new category for Sustainable Finance on SAP Continuous Influence Program (CIP).

The category is dedicated to SAP Green Ledger scenarios with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and is now available in the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, Finance Session.

With this new category, you can now submit Customer Influence requests for SAP Green Ledger related topics and vote for existing requests directly in the portal. This gives you the opportunity to influence future product enhancements and helps SAP better understand and prioritize requirements in the area of Sustainable Finance. In addition, the SAP CIP provides transparency into submitted requests and their status, allowing you to track which requests are under review, planned, or already delivered.

We look forward to shaping the future of SAP Green Ledger together with you!

You can now find the new “Sustainable Finance” category on SAP CIP for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, Finance

entry.title: Are you treating SAP Business Network like a checkbox? You may be missing a huge growth opportunity
entry.content:
Are you already sitting on one of your most valuable digital channels, and barely using it?  

We answer this question in episode 2 of B2B Exchange, our video series for suppliers serious about growing their business.  
 
For this discussion, we’re joined by Justin King, Director at the B2B eCommerce Association, author, speaker, and one of the most respected voices in digital e-commerce. We break down how and why companies should treat their B2B platform profile as a living sales and marketing asset, not just a place to manage transactions.   

Digital is bigger than e-commerce 

As Justin tells us, your customers are on B2B platforms to do their job quickly and efficiently. And that significantly influences how you should think about your digital strategy. 

Instead of asking “how do I build a better website for my products?”, think about “how can I make it easier for new and existing customers to do their job across every channel?”  

That includes evaluating content and keywords on your website, as well as on your marketplaces, social media profiles, and B2B platforms like SAP Business Network.   

The mistake many sellers still make  

Signing up to a B2B Network, adding basic company information, and making a transaction are essential first steps to expanding your channel mix and increasing revenue. But by stopping there, you’re leaving a lot of value on the table.  

Justin explains that joining a B2B network shouldn’t be something you only do because your buyer asked. You can view this as a new strategic growth opportunity for your business.  
 
To make it easier for buyers to find your products, trust your company and complete a purchase, you must be present where they are, and continuously optimize those channels to ensure you stay relevant and competitive. Examples include keeping your company information up to date, testing branding, and experimenting with keywords.  

Watch the new full episode  

Watch Embracing B2B platforms to grow your business, for advice you can use to improve your SAP Business Network presence today. And if you’re after more insights, watch our episode with SAP client Unite and subscribe to the SAP YouTube channel to receive notifications when new B2B Exchange episodes are released.

entry.title: ABAP AI: Creating Custom Generative AI Scenarios in SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
Artificial Intelligence in ABAP – Ready for Today

Artificial Intelligence capabilities are no longer limited to external services or side-by-side extensions. With recent SAP S/4HANA releases, AI scenarios can be consumed and modeled directly within ABAP, allowing existing applications and processes to be enhanced with Generative AI in an enterprise-ready way.

At the center of this enablement is the ABAP AI SDK powered by Intelligent Scenario Lifecycle Management (ISLM). It provides the runtime APIs and lifecycle concepts required to define, manage, and consume AI scenarios from ABAP code in a consistent manner. This makes it possible for customers and partners to build Custom AI scenarios inside their own SAP S/4HANA systems, closely aligned with business logic and system architecture.

Why ABAP AI SDK and ISLM Matter

The ABAP AI SDK powered by ISLM, enables ABAP developers to interact with Generative AI models without embedding provider-specific logic into applications. ISLM acts as the central framework and lifecycle component for AI scenarios, while the ABAP AI SDK exposes standardized APIs to consume these scenarios at runtime.

Beyond pure large language model (LLM) interactions, SAP AI Core provides an orchestration service. Orchestration in SAP AI Core is a managed service that enables unified access, control, and execution of generative AI models through standardized APIs, prompt templating, and configurable AI workflow components.

Using orchestration, LLM interactions can be enriched with additional capabilities such as data masking, content filtering, document grounding, and translation. The ABAP AI SDK powered by ISLM in ABAP can leverage both direct LLM interactions and orchestration features, allowing ABAP applications to consume advanced AI workflows while keeping governance and lifecycle management aligned with SAP standards. 

These capabilities are available as of SAP S/4 HANA 2023.

Learn by Doing: An SAP Developer Mission

To provide a structured, hands-on entry point, we offer an SAP Developer Mission that guides you through the setup and usage of Custom AI scenarios in ABAP.

The mission consists of a tutorial group, guiding you how to set up your SAP S/4 HANA system for custom AI scenarios and dedicated tutorials demonstrating how Custom GenAI scenarios are built and consumed from ABAP using the ABAP AI SDK, including orchestration-based interactions with generative models.

Who Is This Relevant For?

The mission is intended for:

  • ABAP developers who want to understand how Generative AI can be consumed from ABAP
  • Architects evaluating AI integration options for SAP S/4HANA
  • Development teams exploring Custom AI scenarios within their existing SAP landscapes

No prior AI expertise is required, but familiarity with ABAP development is assumed.

Getting Started

Ready? Let's explore the SAP Developer Mission 

👉Get Started with GenAI Development in SAP S/4HANA 

which provides a guided and practical introduction on  Generative AI scenario creation and consumption in ABAP.

Want to Learn More?

  • SAP Help Portal: ABAP AI & ISLM Documentation
  • SAP Community Page: Intelligent Scenario Lifecycle Management for SAP S/4HANA 
  • SAP Community Blog: Scaling Generative AI in SAP S/4HANA Enterprise-Ready Innovations with ISLM 
  • SAP Community Blog: Developing AI‑enabled applications with the ABAP AI SDK (Public Cloud & Steampunk)

entry.title: Re: SAP Analytics Cloud Activities Log Command-line Interface (CLI) to Automate Downloads + Associat
entry.content:
Could you share the details of the failure, please? Are you sure it's not due to a documented 'known issue' (page 23 of the user guide)

The downloaded content for 'lastfullday' will differ between manual download and the API sample script. The manual download will be smaller than via the API sample script, so the two aren't comparable.  You could try a manual download of the last week (since more than the last day is actually downloaded when using 'lastfullday' via my API sample script). 

Please also follow the 'Troubleshooting' section of the user guide and enable the additional logging as mentioned there.

I've not been able to reproduce the issue, but I'm not surprised, as I believe it's the 'content' of what you're downloading that's causing the issue.

Kind regards Matthew

entry.title: Issue with Event-Based Production Costing: Goods Receipt Value
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

We are currently facing an issue in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition related to Event-Based Production Costing and inventory valuation.

In the app “Analyze Production Costs - Event-Based”, the production/process order costs are calculated correctly based on:

  • Material consumption

  • Labor/activity costs

  • Other production postings

However, when posting the Goods Receipt from the production/process order, the value posted in “Material Documents Overview” is incorrect.

 

Observed behavior:

  • In some cases, the finished product is valuated at 0.

  • In other cases, the posted value is different from the cost calculated in “Analyze Production Costs - Event-Based”.

  • Semi-finished products also do not receive the expected calculated cost.

Example:
Manufacturing Order: 1000069
Difference between consumed and produced value: 939,533.19 MAD

This issue has a major impact on:

  • Inventory valuation

  • Moving Average Price calculation

  • Financial balances and production costing

Additional Information:

  • Event-Based Production Costing is active

  • No errors found in “Postprocess Event-Based Postings - Product Costing”

  • Price Control used: Moving Average Price (MAP)

My question:
What configuration or business settings should be checked to ensure that the costs calculated in “Analyze Production Costs - Event-Based” are correctly reflected in Goods Receipt postings and Material Documents?

Has anyone faced a similar issue with Event-Based Production Costing and MAP valuation?

Any guidance or recommendations would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you.

entry.title: Re: SQL Error in HANA Studio Seems to be Cached
entry.content:
Hi,

This error you describe “could not split statements; sql console content is too large" is a very typical occurrence when executing a "HANA_Configuration_MiniChecks_2.00.085+.txt" report for example.

However the statement will typically keep running without fail if you check the green progress bar on the bottom right hand side in HANA Studio. So it is usually more of a warning and an actual exception failure.

I have encountered this many times during Mini check execution and the statement never actually failed because of this error, so I would suggest monitoring. If the statement does fail it will likely show an updated error reason in the same "result" pane where the original error appears.

Kind Regards
Simon
